title: A muted arrow crossed out by a bold crimson X. The arrow is what was attempted; the X is what the contract made impossible. Invalid states don't fail at runtime in Precept — they are forbidden at definition time by the structural absence of a declared transition. The X is not an error message; it IS the prevention.
conceptualBridge: The forbidden crossing means Precept's prevention because the X is not a runtime error indicator but a structural declaration that this transition was never made possible — and therefore can never occur.
decision: Rejected: avg 3.94 below threshold — the X-over-arrow silhouette is heavily colonized by delete/close/error icons in UI design; the muted arrow disappears in monochrome leaving just a generic X; the 'prevention' concept is sound but this form doesn't communicate 'structural prevention' vs. 'runtime error'.
